content.problemStatement:
Chef has finished his freshman year in college. As a present, his parents gave him a new problem to solve:



Chef has to fill a **K x K** square grid of integers in a certain way. Let us say that such a grid is valid if:


Each cell contains an integer from **1** and **K** (inclusive). 
   No integer appears twice in the same row or the same column.

Let **F(K)** be maximum possible distance between the center of the square and the closest cell that contains **1**, among all possible squares with the side length **K**.



Here, we use the following notions:


 The distance between cell **(x, y)** and **(i, j)** is equal to **|x-i|+|y-j|**. 
   The center of a **K x K** square is cell **((K+1)/2, (K+1)/2)** for odd **K**.
content.inputFormat: The first line of input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing a single odd integer K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the K x K grid with the maximum possible distance between the center of the square and the closest cell that contains 1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For K = 1, the only possible grid is [[1]]. For K = 3, one possible grid is [[3, 2, 1], [1, 3, 2], [2, 1, 3]].

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a backtracking approach to construct the grid. We start by placing the number 1 in the center of the grid and then try to place the remaining numbers in the grid such that no number appears twice in the same row or column. We use a recursive function to try all possible placements of the numbers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(K^2 * T) — the time complexity is dominated by the construction of the grid for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K^2) — the space complexity is dominated by the storage of the grid.
